title: Implement Role-Based Access Control for ShopFlow
description: Add role-based authorization to an existing authenticated ASP.NET Core application. Implement Admin, Manager, and Customer roles with different access permissions for ShopFlow e-commerce platform.
instructions:
You have an ASP.NET Core 9.0 app with authentication configured but no authorization. Implement a role-based access control system for ShopFlow!

**Requirements:**

1. **Configure Roles in Program.cs:**
   - Add role services to DI container with AddIdentity<ApplicationUser, IdentityRole>()
   - Include AddRoles<IdentityRole>() to enable role management
   - Configure role-based authorization policies

2. **Create Role-Protected Endpoints:**
   - GET /api/admin/dashboard - Only accessible to "Admin" role
   - GET /api/manager/inventory - Accessible to "Admin" and "Manager" roles
   - GET /api/customer/orders - Accessible to "Customer" and "Admin" roles
   - POST /api/admin/users/promote - Only "Admin" can promote users to Manager
   - DELETE /api/admin/products/{id} - Only "Admin" can delete products

3. **Add Role Seeding:**
   - Create a seeder that ensures roles exist: Admin, Manager, Customer
   - Create a default admin user with Admin role
   - Create a test manager user

4. **Handle Edge Cases:**
   - Return 403 Forbidden when user lacks required role
   - Return 401 Unauthorized when not authenticated
   - Include helpful error messages with role requirements

**Scenarios to Support:**
- Admin: Full access to all endpoints including user management and product deletion
- Manager: Can access inventory management but cannot delete products or manage users
- Customer: Can only access their own orders, cannot access admin or manager endpoints

**Hint:** Use [Authorize(Roles = "...")] for declarative authorization. Multiple roles use comma-separated values for OR logic.

testCases.description: Should configure AddIdentity with role support
Should have Authorize attribute with role restrictions
Should handle 403 Forbidden for unauthorized roles

hints.text: Use builder.Services.AddIdentity<ApplicationUser, IdentityRole>().AddRoles<IdentityRole>() to enable role support.
Apply [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")] attribute to protect admin-only endpoints declaratively.
For multiple roles with OR logic, use [Authorize(Roles = "Admin,Manager")] - any of these roles is sufficient.
Use RoleManager<IdentityRole> to check and create roles in your seeder class.
Return Results.Forbid() when the user is authenticated but lacks the required role - this sends HTTP 403.

commonMistakes.consequence: Role services won't be registered. You'll get runtime errors when checking roles.
commonMistakes.correction: Make sure to chain .AddRoles<IdentityRole>() after AddIdentity().

commonMistakes.mistake: Using AND logic instead of OR for multiple roles
commonMistakes.consequence: [Authorize(Roles = "Admin,User")] means Admin OR User, not Admin AND User.
commonMistakes.correction: For AND logic, apply multiple Authorize attributes or use a policy with multiple role requirements.

commonMistakes.mistake: Checking roles manually instead of using attributes
commonMistakes.consequence: More verbose code, harder to maintain, inconsistent security patterns.
commonMistakes.correction: Prefer [Authorize(Roles = "...")] for simple scenarios. It's declarative and self-documenting.

commonMistakes.consequence: Clients can't distinguish between 'not logged in' and 'logged in but not authorized'.
commonMistakes.correction: Return 401 for unauthenticated users, 403 for authenticated users without proper roles.
